title: Spotify
text:

Objective: To analyze and explore Spotify audio features to understand the characteristics and patterns present in different songs. # #

Data Dictionary

Quantitative Data

Acousticness (dbl): A measure (0–1) of how likely the track is acoustic.
Danceability (dbl): A measure (0–1) of how suitable the track is for dancing.
Duration (int): The length of the song in milliseconds.
Energy (dbl): The intensity and activity level of the song (0–1).
Instrumentalness (dbl): The likelihood (0–1) that the track contains no vocals.
Key (int): The musical key of the track, represented numerically (0–11).
Liveness (dbl): The probability (0–1) that the track was performed live.
Loudness (dbl): The overall loudness of the track in decibels (dB).
Mode (int): Indicates whether the song is in a major (1) or minor (0) key.
Speechiness (dbl): The presence of spoken words in a track (0–1).
Tempo (dbl): The speed of the track in beats per minute (BPM).
Time Signature (int): The number of beats per bar in the track.
Valence (dbl): The musical positivity of the track (0–1), where higher values sound happier.
Popularity (int): A score from 0–100 indicating how popular the song is on Spotify.

Qualitative Data

Artist Name (string): The name of the artist(s) who performed the track.
Track Name (string): The title of the song.
Track ID (string): A unique identifier assigned to each song by Spotify.

       1 
20.85283 



Summary of Inferences

I found this dataset interesting because I never knew how many factors affect the success of a song. I converted duration from milliseconds to minutes. Made me question why they even recorded it in milliseconds because it is so hard to compare it that way. Tempo and popularity showed a very slight linear relation, so I discarded it. Popularity vs duration showed a slight inverse relation, showing that longer songs aren’t as popular. Popularity vs loudness showed a significant linear relation so I built the first model using this. I also tried creating a model of [popularity vs (loudness, instrumentalness and danceability)]. The adjusted R^2 values of both models are pretty low but the second model proves to be more accurate than the first.

Data Dictionary

Quantitative Data

Step Count (int): The total number of steps taken during the day.
Distance Travelled (dbl): The total distance covered (in km).
Active Time (dbl): The total amount of time spent in physical activity (in minutes).
Flights Climbed (int): The number of floors or flights of stairs ascended.

title: Bird Diversity
text:
Bird genetic diversity study of 387 bird species





Objective: Ecologists and evolutionary biologists have long investigated links between diversity and geography. For example, as the area of a habitat increases, the number of different species found within it increases—a seemingly obvious finding, but the exact shape of the relationship has been found to relate to the rates of immigration and extinction in the habitat, predator-prey behaviors, and so on.
A similar relationship would seem likely to exist within a species. A species that lives over a small area might have low genetic diversity, because individuals frequently interbreed and there is little opportunity for some individuals to evolve separately from others. A species that lives over a wide area might have high genetic diversity, both because the varied habitat in which it lives means different individuals experience different natural selection pressures, and because individuals far apart may never be able to interbreed.
The exact nature of this relationship could be used to test hypotheses about evolution and natural selection. This dataset comes from a paper that explored the relationship in birds, which have widely varying geographic ranges and hence could be a good group to work with. # #

#                 Width, color = 'hotpink') %>%
#   gf_lm(color = "black")%>%
#   gf_labs(title = "Weight vs (Average Length, Height, Width)",
#           x = "Model",
#           y = "Weight (grams)")


Predicting Model


test = data.frame(Average_Length = 30,
                  Height = 15,
                  Width = 5.5)

predict(fish_lm, newdata = test)

       1 
540.5573 



Summary of Inferences

This dataset contains three recordings of the length of each fish. I used the average of these lengths for the graphs. I checked the correlation of [average length vs weight], [width vs weight], and [height vs weight] and I found that there was no linear relation between any of them. However, I created a linear regression model of [weight vs (average length, height and width)] and the summary shows that it is very significant. The adjusted R^2 value is closer to 1, thus proves that this linear regression model is successful. However, I was not able to plot the graph. On learning what the error was, I came to know that this was a multiple linear regression model and the syntax for the graph was incorrect.

title: Lake Placid Women’s Ironman Triathlon
text:
The data set looks at female participants in the triathlon Ironman





Objective: The motivation for this data analysis is to explore the relationships between swim times, bike times, and run times (in minutes) in order to gain insights into the performance patterns of the athletes. By analyzing these relationships, we can understand the interplay between different segments of the race and potentially identify areas of improvement for athletes. # #

Setting up R Packages

library(ggformula)
library(janitor)
library(naniar)
library(visdat)


Reading Data

Data Dictionary

Quantitative Data

Bib (int): registration number of each runner used for identification
Division.Rank (int): Within the divisions, the place each runner has obtained over all races
Overall.Time (dbl): The total time it took to complete the Ironman in minutes
Overall.Rank (int): The runner’s finishing place for that particular triathlon
Swim.Time (dbl): The time in minutes it took to complete the swimming portion
Swim.Rank (int): The place the runner finished for the swim portion
Bike.Time (dbl): The time in minutes it took to complete the biking portion
Bike.Rank (int): The place the runner finished for the bike portion
Run.Time (dbl): The time in minutes it took to complete the running portion
Run.Rank (int): The place the runner finished for the running portion
Year (int): The year when the mentioned participant ran

Qualitative Data

Name (fct): The participant’s name
Country (fct): What country the participant is from
Gender (fct): The participant’s gender
Division (fct): The age range or membership a runner is in
Finish.Status (fct): States whether someone completed the Ironman successfully
Location (fct): Where the Ironman takes place

Summary of Inferences

The Ironman triathlon is held in this particular order- swimming, biking, and finally running. The order as well as the distance of these sports highly affects the data. In this dataset, the graphs for run completion times and bike completion times are normal data distributions. However, the graph for swim completion times is very odd. Almost every participant has completed the swim around the same time. On checking the correlation of different attributes, I found that the relation between [run time and overall time] and [bike time and overall time] were linear, so I used them to make two prediction models. Swim time was not so helpful.
